title: 31th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2015 — Écolier (Grades 3-4)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

questions.question_text: See PDF page 1 for the figure.
questions.solution: See PDF page 1 for the figure. The answer is 15.
solution_steps: Examine the figure on PDF page 1.
The correct count or value from the figure is 15.

questions.question_text: Eric had 10 equal metal strips. He has screwed pairs of them together into five long strips. Which strip is the longest?
questions.solution: See PDF page 1 for the image of the five strips. Strip A has the smallest overlap when the two component strips are screwed together, so it results in the longest combined strip.
solution_steps: Each long strip is made by screwing two equal shorter strips together.
The total length depends on how much the two strips overlap at the connection point.
Less overlap means a longer resulting strip.
From the figure, strip A has the least overlap, making it the longest.

questions.question_text: Which number is hidden behind the square?
questions.solution: The figure on PDF page 1 shows an arithmetic expression with a square hiding one number. Solving the equation gives the hidden number as 6.
solution_steps: Look at the equation shown in the figure on PDF page 1.
Substitute each option and check which value makes the equation true.
The hidden number is 6.

questions.question_text: We start drawing a line at every second dot on the circle until we are back at the number 1. The first two lines are drawn already. What figure do we get?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the circle diagram. By connecting every second dot around the circle, the resulting figure matches option E.
solution_steps: The circle has dots labeled around it; lines are drawn by skipping every other dot.
Continue the pattern: from the current position, skip one dot and draw a line to the next.
Repeat until returning to dot 1.
The completed shape matches the figure shown in option E.

questions.question_text: Lucy had some kangas in her wallet (see the picture). She went to a shop where she bought a ball and paid 7 kangas. How many kangas did she have when she left the shop?
questions.solution: See PDF pages 2–3 for the wallet image showing Lucy's kangas. After paying 7 kangas for the ball, the remaining amount in the wallet matches option B.
solution_steps: Count the total number of kangas shown in Lucy's wallet on PDF page 2.
Subtract 7 (the price of the ball).
The remaining amount corresponds to the wallet shown in option B.

questions.question_text: A whole number has two digits. The product of the digits of this number is 15. The sum of the digits of this number is:
questions.solution: The two digits must multiply to 15. The only single-digit factor pair of 15 is 3 × 5. Their sum is 3 + 5 = 8.
solution_steps: List factor pairs of 15 using single digits (1–9): only 3 × 5 = 15.
The pair 1 × 15 doesn't work since 15 is not a single digit.
The two digits are 3 and 5.

questions.question_text: In the figure, we see an island with a highly indented coastline and several frogs. How many of these frogs are sitting on the island?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the island figure. By carefully tracing the indented coastline, exactly 6 frogs are positioned on the land portion of the island.
solution_steps: Trace the coastline carefully in the figure on PDF page 3, noting all the inlets and peninsulas.
Determine whether each frog is on the land (inside the coastline) or in the water (outside).
Count only the frogs that are on the land: there are 6.

questions.question_text: My umbrella has KANGAROO written on top. It is shown in the picture on the right. Which of the following pictures also shows my umbrella?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the umbrella images. When viewed from above at different angles, only option A shows the umbrella in a valid orientation (rotation without flipping) that matches the original.
solution_steps: The reference umbrella shows KANGAROO written in a specific direction when viewed from above.
An umbrella can be rotated but not flipped (mirrored) when viewed from the same side.
Check each option: eliminate any that are mirror images of the original.
Option A is the only one that can be obtained by rotating the original umbrella.

questions.question_text: Basil wants to cut the shape depicted in Figure 1 into identical triangles as in Figure 2. How many triangles will he get?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for Figures 1 and 2. The shape in Figure 1 can be divided into exactly 15 triangles of the type shown in Figure 2.
solution_steps: Identify the unit triangle size from Figure 2 on PDF page 4.
Count how many such unit triangles fit into the larger shape in Figure 1.
The total count is 15 triangles.

questions.question_text: Luis has 7 apples and 2 bananas. He gives 2 apples to Yuri who, in return gives bananas to Luis. Then Luis has as many apples as bananas. How many bananas did Yuri give to Luis?
questions.solution: After giving away 2 apples, Luis has 5 apples. For Luis to have equal apples and bananas, he needs 5 bananas. He already has 2, so Yuri gave him 3.
solution_steps: Luis starts with 7 apples and 2 bananas.
He gives 2 apples to Yuri: Luis now has 5 apples and 2 bananas.
For equal apples and bananas, Luis needs 5 bananas total.
He already has 2 bananas, so Yuri must give him 5 − 2 = 3 bananas.

questions.question_text: Jack built a cube using 27 small cubes which are colored either black or white (see figure). No two of the small cubes which are colored in the same color have a common face. How many white cubes did Jack use?
questions.solution: In a 3×3×3 checkerboard coloring, positions with coordinate sum (i+j+k) even get one color and odd sum get the other. There are 14 even-sum and 13 odd-sum positions. From the figure, the corners (even-sum positions) are black, so white cubes number 13.
solution_steps: Label each small cube position (i,j,k) with i,j,k ∈ {0,1,2}.
Color: black if i+j+k is even, white if i+j+k is odd.
Count positions with even sum: 8 all-even + 6 two-odd-one-even = 14 black cubes.
Count positions with odd sum: 27 − 14 = 13 white cubes.
From the figure, the corner cubes are black, confirming white = 13.

questions.question_text: In a speed skating competition 10 racers reached the final. Tom overtook 3 racers more than overtook him. Which place did Tom end up in?
questions.solution: If Tom finishes in place p, he overtook (10 − p) racers and (p − 1) racers overtook him. Setting (10 − p) − (p − 1) = 3 gives p = 4.
solution_steps: Let Tom's finishing place be p (out of 10 racers).
Number Tom overtook = racers finishing behind him = 10 − p.
Number who overtook Tom = racers finishing ahead = p − 1.
Tom finished in 4th place.

questions.question_text: Josip has 4 toys — a car, a doll, a ball and a ship. He wants to put them on a line on a shelf. The ship has to be next to the car and the doll has to be next to the car. In how many ways can he arrange them so that all the conditions are fulfilled?
questions.solution: The car must be adjacent to both the ship and the doll, so it must sit between them. The three toys form the block Ship–Car–Doll or Doll–Car–Ship (2 orderings). The ball can go at either end: 2 × 2 = 4 arrangements.
solution_steps: Ship and doll both must be next to car, so car is in the middle of the three: S–C–D or D–C–S.
This gives a block of 3 toys in 2 orderings.
The ball can be placed at the beginning or end of this block: 2 positions.
Total arrangements: 2 × 2 = 4.

questions.question_text: Pete rides a bicycle in the park as in the figure. He starts from the point S in the direction of the arrow. At the first crossroad he turns right, then at the next crossroad he turns left, then right again, then left again and so on in that order. What is the sign at which he won't pass?
questions.solution: See PDF page 5 for the park map. Tracing Pete's route with the alternating right–left turn pattern, he passes signs A, B, C, and E, but never reaches the sign shown in option D.
solution_steps: Start at point S and follow the arrow direction on PDF page 5.
At crossroad 1: turn right.
At crossroad 2: turn left.
Continue alternating right/left at each crossroad.
Trace the full route and note which sign is never encountered — it is option D.

questions.question_text: There are 5 ladybugs (see fig.). Two ladybugs are friends with each other if the numbers of spots that they have differ exactly by 1. On Kangaroo Day each of the ladybugs sent to each of her friends one SMS greeting. How many SMS greetings were sent?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the ladybug figure showing their spot counts. From the figure there are exactly 3 friendship pairs (ladybugs whose spot counts differ by 1). Each pair exchanges 2 SMS (one each way), giving 3 × 2 = 6 total.
solution_steps: Read the number of spots on each of the 5 ladybugs from PDF page 6.
Identify all pairs whose spot counts differ by exactly 1 — there are 3 such pairs.
Each friendship pair sends 2 SMS (A→B and B→A).
Total SMS = 3 × 2 = 6.

questions.question_text: The figure is divided into three identical pieces. What did the pieces look like?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the figure. The shape can be divided into exactly three congruent pieces, and each piece matches the shape shown in option A.
solution_steps: Examine the figure on PDF page 6.
Try dividing it into three equal parts.
Each resulting piece matches option A in shape and size.

questions.question_text: Luisa wants to fold a cube from a paper net. By mistake she drew 7 squares on her sheet instead of 6 squares. Which square must she remove so that the figure remains connected and Luisa can fold a cube from it?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the net with numbered squares. Removing square 3 leaves a valid connected net of 6 squares that folds into a cube. The remaining squares after removing 3 form one of the standard 11 cube nets.
solution_steps: A cube net requires exactly 6 squares, and the net must be connected.
Try removing each of the 7 squares and check if the remaining 6 form a valid cube net.
Removing square 3 keeps the net connected and produces a shape that folds into a cube.
Removing the other squares either disconnects the net or creates an invalid folding pattern.

questions.question_text: We have three transparent sheets with the following patterns. We can only rotate the three sheets without turning over. Then we put them exactly on top of each other. What is the maximum possible number of black squares seen in the obtained square if looked at from above?
questions.solution: See PDF page 7 for the three sheet patterns. By choosing optimal rotations (0°, 90°, 180°, or 270° each) for the three sheets, the maximum number of distinct black squares visible from above is 8.
solution_steps: Each sheet has some black squares on a grid; sheets can be rotated in 90° increments.
Try all combinations of rotations for the three sheets.
A square appears black in the combined view if at least one sheet has it black in that position.
The optimal combination of rotations results in 8 black squares visible.

questions.question_text: The numbers 2, 3, 5, 6 and 7 are written in the squares of the cross (see fig.) so that the sum of the numbers in the row is equal to the sum of the numbers in the column. Which of the numbers can be written in the center square of the cross?
questions.solution: The total sum is 2+3+5+6+7=23. If the center is c, the remaining 4 numbers split into two pairs (row arms and column arms) each summing to (23−c)/2. For this to be a whole number, c must be odd. Testing odd candidates: c=3 gives no valid pair split; c=5 gives pairs {2,7} and {3,6}; c=7 gives pairs {2,6} and {3,5}. So center can be 5 or 7.
solution_steps: Total of all five numbers: 2+3+5+6+7 = 23.
With center value c, each arm-pair sums to (23−c)/2, requiring c to be odd.
Odd candidates: 3, 5, 7.
c=3: remaining {2,5,6,7} — no two numbers sum to (23−3)/2=10. Invalid.
c=5: remaining {2,3,6,7} — pairs 2+7=9 and 3+6=9. Valid.
c=7: remaining {2,3,5,6} — pairs 2+6=8 and 3+5=8. Valid.
Center can be 5 or 7.

questions.question_text: Peter has ten balls, numbered from 0 to 9. He distributed these balls among three friends: John got three balls, George four and Ann three. Then he asked each of his friends to multiply the numbers on the balls they got and the results were: 0 for John, 72 for George and 90 for Ann. What is the sum of the numbers on the balls that John received?
questions.solution: John's product is 0, so John has ball 0. Ball 5 must go to Ann (it's the only way to get factor 5 in 90). Ann's set is either {2,5,9} or {3,5,6}. In both cases, George's 4 balls multiply to 72, and John's remaining two balls are 7 and 8. John's sum = 0+7+8 = 15.
solution_steps: John's product = 0, so John holds ball 0.
Ann's product = 90 = 2×5×9 or 3×5×6; ball 5 must be with Ann.
Case 1: Ann = {2,5,9}. George picks 4 from {1,3,4,6,7,8} with product 72: {1,3,4,6} works (1×3×4×6=72). John = {0,7,8}.
Case 2: Ann = {3,5,6}. George picks 4 from {1,2,4,7,8,9} with product 72: {1,2,4,9} works (1×2×4×9=72). John = {0,7,8}.
In both cases John has {0,7,8}; sum = 0+7+8 = 15.

questions.question_text: Three ropes are laid down on the floor as shown. You can make one big, complete loop with three other pieces of rope. Which of the ropes shown will give you one big loop?
questions.solution: See PDF page 8 for the rope figures. Analyzing the topology of each rope arrangement, only option C, when connected at its free ends with three extra pieces, forms a single large closed loop rather than multiple separate loops.
solution_steps: Examine each rope arrangement on PDF page 8.
For a single big loop, all crossings must be connected so that tracing the path never splits or repeats before completing the loop.
Test each option by tracing the path including the three connector pieces.
Only option C produces one single complete loop.

questions.question_text: The figure shows a dotted sheet. Both horizontally and vertically the distance from one point to the next is equal. Let four points at the time become vertices in different squares. How many squares with different area is it possible to make?
questions.solution: On a 4×4 dot grid, squares can be axis-aligned or tilted. The possible distinct areas are: 1 (1×1), 2 (tilted, side √2), 4 (2×2), 5 (tilted, side √5), and 9 (3×3). That gives 5 different areas.
solution_steps: Axis-aligned squares: 1×1 (area 1), 2×2 (area 4), 3×3 (area 9) — all fit on the grid.
Tilted square with vector (1,1): side = √2, area = 2. Fits on the grid.
Tilted square with vector (1,2) or (2,1): side = √5, area = 5. Fits on the 4×4 grid.
Tilted square with vector (2,2): side = 2√2, area = 8. Does not fit in a 4×4 dot grid.
Total distinct areas: 1, 2, 4, 5, 9 — five different areas.

questions.question_text: Tom draws a shark, a cat and a rhino and cuts them in three pieces each as shown. Then he can make different animals by combining one head, one middle part and one bottom. How many different fantasy and real animals can Tom create?
questions.solution: There are 3 choices for head, 3 for middle, and 3 for bottom. The total number of combinations is 3 × 3 × 3 = 27, which includes the 3 real animals and 24 fantasy combinations.
solution_steps: Each animal is cut into 3 parts: head, middle, bottom.
From 3 animals we get 3 heads, 3 middles, and 3 bottoms.
Any combination is allowed: 3 choices for head × 3 for middle × 3 for bottom.
Total = 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 different animals (real and fantasy).

questions.question_text: Anna, Berta, Charlie, David and Elisa were baking cookies during the weekend. Over the whole weekend Anna made 24 cookies, Berta 25, Charlie 26, David 27 and Elisa 28. After the whole weekend one of them had twice as many cookies as after Saturday, one 3 times, one 4 times, one 5 times and one 6 times as many. Who baked the most cookies on Saturday?
questions.solution: Each person's Saturday total equals their weekend total divided by their multiplier. Finding which multipliers give whole numbers: Berta=25/5=5, Charlie=26/2=13, David=27/3=9, Elisa=28/4=7, Anna=24/6=4. Charlie baked the most on Saturday with 13 cookies.
solution_steps: Test each multiplier (2,3,4,5,6) against each person's total.
Berta: 25÷5=5 (only integer). Berta's multiplier is 5.
Charlie: 26÷2=13 (only integer). Charlie's multiplier is 2.
David: 27÷3=9 (only integer). David's multiplier is 3.
Elisa: 28÷4=7 (only integer). Elisa's multiplier is 4.
Anna: 24÷6=4 (remaining multiplier). Anna's multiplier is 6.
Saturday totals: Anna=4, Berta=5, Charlie=13, David=9, Elisa=7.
Charlie baked the most cookies on Saturday (13).
